title: Contract Management
description: Master contract rules, contract lifecycle, and the relationship between contracts and opportunities.

sections.title: Contract Management
sections.content:
Contracts are the legal foundation of customer relationships. A Quote is an offer. A Contract is a commitment—a binding agreement that governs terms, pricing, scope, warranty, support, and all the legal conditions that matter. This is where risk, revenue, and obligations live. Handle it carefully.

Contract vs. Quote: Know the Difference

Quote = proposal, not binding, can expire ("valid for 30 days")

Contract = legally binding, enforceable, creates mutual obligations, no expiration unless a term is specified

A Quote becomes a Contract when accepted and signed. The Contract incorporates the Quote's pricing and scope but adds legal language, terms, and enforcement mechanisms.

Core Contract Principles

Link to the Right Account: Contracts link to the Entity Child Account (the one delivering/consuming service), not the Parent Account. Always link to the underlying Opportunity and Quote too. Correct linkage ensures accurate revenue attribution and full deal traceability.

Contract Status Is Separate: The Contract has its own lifecycle status, independent of the Opportunity stage. While an Opportunity shows "Closed - Won," the Contract moves through Draft → Reviewed → Executed → Active → Renewed/Expired. This separation lets you track the legal status independently.

Contract Status Lifecycle

- Draft: Being prepared by legal/contracting, not yet ready for customer
- In Review: Internal review by legal, finance, or compliance for standards compliance
- Sent to Customer: Approved internally, delivered for customer review
- In Customer Review: Customer reviewing, may request modifications, circulating for their approvals
- Execution Pending: Ready to sign, awaiting authorized signatures
- Executed: Signed by authorized reps from both sides—now legally binding
- Active: Executed and customer is consuming services under the agreement
- Renewed: Contract term extended or renewed for additional period
- Expired: Contract term ended, customer no longer covered
- Terminated: Ended early by mutual agreement or breach

Required Information

Every Contract must have:

- Customer details (Account, Contact)
- Linked Opportunity and Quote
- Effective and expiration dates
- Products and services defined
- Pricing and payment terms
- Service level agreements (if applicable)
- Warranty and support terms
- Termination provisions
- Governing law and dispute resolution
- Authorized signatory names and titles from both sides

Signing Authority

Only authorized signatories can sign. Your company defines approval levels—maybe individual contributors up to $250K, managers up to $1M, executives above. The Contract record documents who signed and confirms they had authority.

Signature Authority Validation: After signing, validate that signatories were authorized. Contracts signed by unauthorized personnel aren't enforceable.

Legal Review Is Non-Negotiable

Every contract goes to legal before customer delivery. Legal ensures:

- Compliance with applicable laws
- Protection of company interests
- Alignment with company contracting standards
- No conflicting terms vs. company policy

Customers requesting special terms (extended payment, limited liability, non-standard warranties) need appropriate approval before inclusion.

Contract Lifecycle Events

Contract Execution: Deal closes Won, Account moves to Onboarding, revenue recognition may begin.

Contract Renewal: As expiration approaches, account manager initiates renewal conversations. New contract with updated terms and pricing.

Contract Amendment: Customer wants to change pricing, scope, or terms? Draft an amendment, approve it, execute it. Amend existing contracts; don't overwrite them.

Upsell/Expansion: Adding new products or services? New contract or amendment required.

Non-Renewal/Termination: No renewal or early termination? Update status to Expired or Terminated, move Account to Dormant (or Blocked if issues exist).

Salesforce Tracking

Contract records include:

- Customer info (Account, Contact, Billing Address)
- Dates (Start, End, Renewal Notice Period)
- Signature details (date, authorized signatories)
- Linked Opportunity and Quote
- Products and Pricing (sometimes line items)
- Special Terms (discounts, credits, non-standard conditions)
- Auto-Renewal settings
- Renewal Reminder dates (alerts account manager)

Best Practices

- Keep Distinct: Quote for sales, Contract for binding. Don't conflate them.
- Link Correctly: Always link to correct Entity Child Account, Opportunity, and Quote.
- Get Approvals: Never send to customer without legal and approval review.
- Document Amendments: Create amendment documents, track in Salesforce. Never overwrite original.
- Monitor Renewals: Set reminders so renewal conversations start well ahead of expiration.
- Maintain Authority Records: Document signing authority and approval levels. Protects company from unauthorized commitments.
- Secure Storage: Fully executed contracts stored securely, accessible only to authorized personnel.

Contracts end the sales phase and start the fulfillment phase. They protect your company, set clear expectations with customers, and lay the foundation for long-term success. Get them right.

keyTakeaways: Contracts are legally binding agreements linked to Entity Child Accounts, with their own lifecycle status distinct from Opportunity stage
Required information includes effective/expiration dates, products, pricing, service levels, warranty, and authorized signatories
Contract review by Legal ensures compliance and that signing authority is validated before execution
